content:
Machine learning grew out of a simple frustration: **hand-coded rules do not scale** when inputs are noisy, high-dimensional, or constantly changing. Early AI (1950s–1980s) relied on symbolic logic and expert systems—if-then rules curated by specialists. Those systems worked in narrow domains but were brittle and expensive to maintain.

**Arthur Samuel** coined the phrase *machine learning* in 1959 for checkers programs that improved by learning from self-play—an early example of learning from experience rather than static rules. **Frank Rosenblatt**'s perceptron (1957) showed that adjustable weights could learn linear decision boundaries from labeled examples.

The field formalized in the 1980s–1990s: **Tom Mitchell**'s definition (experience E, task T, performance P) gave ML a testable research agenda. **Backpropagation** (popularized in the 1980s) made neural networks trainable; **support vector machines** and **boosting** advanced classical ML on structured data.

The 2000s brought **large labeled datasets** (ImageNet, web text) and cheap compute. **Deep learning** (2012 onward) showed that layered models could learn hierarchical features from raw pixels and tokens. Today **generative AI** extends the same principle—learn parameters from massive corpora to model language and images.

Throughout, the constant thread is unchanged: **replace exhaustive manual rules with parameters fit from data**, then evaluate whether the system generalizes beyond the examples it saw.

timeline.label: Perceptron and early learning (1950s)
timeline.description: Rosenblatt's perceptron and Samuel's checkers learner demonstrated programs improving from experience.

timeline.label: Mitchell formalization (1980s–1997)
timeline.description: Task, experience, and performance measure defined what it means for a program to learn.

timeline.label: Statistical ML maturity (1990s–2000s)
timeline.description: SVMs, boosting, and probabilistic graphical models scaled learning on tabular and text data.

timeline.label: Deep learning scale (2012–present)
timeline.description: GPU training on large datasets made neural networks the default for vision, speech, and language.
